{"step_by_step_thinking": "A continuous variable is one that can take on any value within a range, such as time, weight, or temperature. To represent such data visually, a histogram is the most appropriate choice because it groups data into intervals (bins) and displays the frequency of values within each interval. This allows for the visualization of the distribution of continuous data. In contrast, bar charts (simple or multiple) are typically used for categorical data, where each bar represents a distinct category. A pie diagram is used to show proportions of categorical data, not continuous variables. The documents from MEDCORP and UMLS both support this: in the MEDCORP source, histograms are explicitly mentioned in the context of exploratory data analysis and are used to display the distribution of continuous variables, such as time or drug life, and to identify outliers or normality. For example, Document [18] states that histograms reveal outliers and are used to examine data distribution for continuous variables. Therefore, a histogram is the correct choice for denoting a continuous variable.", "answer_choice": "B"}
